title: Route titles and focus management

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Route titles and focus management; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Route titles and focus management
sources.claim: Client navigation must update document title and move or announce focus context so keyboard and screen-reader users perceive the new view.

sources.locator: React Router Documentation; stable declarative mode accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Route titles and focus management
sources.claim: Client navigation must update document title and move or announce focus context so keyboard and screen-reader users perceive the new view.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Client navigation must update document title and move or announce focus context so keyboard and screen-reader users perceive the new view.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: After a course route changes, the main heading receives managed focus without trapping subsequent navigation. Your goal is to verify route changes are perceivable to keyboard and assistive-technology users. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Remote data and asynchronous UI; javascript browser url; Declarative routes and links; Refs and imperative escape hatches; html metadata title description. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Client navigation must update document title and move or announce focus context so keyboard and screen-reader users perceive the new view. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Focusing every route can disrupt restored history position, dialogs need separate rules, and hash navigation has native expectations. This draft therefore rejects: Changing visible React components automatically announces a new page to assistive technology.

includes: Update document context and move focus appropriately after client navigation.
Verify route changes are perceivable to keyboard and assistive-technology users.
excludes: Focusing every route can disrupt restored history position, dialogs need separate rules, and hash navigation has native expectations.

outcomes: Verify route changes are perceivable to keyboard and assistive-technology users.

examples.title: Route titles and focus management: concrete case
examples.situation: After a course route changes, the main heading receives managed focus without trapping subsequent navigation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: After a course route changes, the main heading receives managed focus without trapping subsequent navigation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Manage title and focus or announcements on client route changes; visual replacement alone may be silent.
examples.result: The result follows because Manage title and focus or announcements on client route changes; visual replacement alone may be silent.

examples.title: Route titles and focus management: boundary case

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Changing visible React components automatically announces a new page to assistive technology.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Manage title and focus or announcements on client route changes; visual replacement alone may be silent.
